text: The surgeon places a trocar at the umbilicus for diagnostic laparoscopy, followed by positioning three robotic operating arms at the umbilical level. An airflow device is positioned along the anterior axillary line near the costal margin. A cut-down technique is used to place an applied port caudal to the umbilicus between the umbilicus and the midclavicular line on the right, which is used by an assistant for tissue extraction.

The surgeon begins the operation by taking down the falciform ligament, followed by opening the gastro-hepatic omentum in a stellate fashion. Dissection proceeds along the common hepatic artery, where an enlarged lymph node is removed. The right gastric artery is clipped twice proximally and divided, followed by the gastroduodenal artery, which is similarly clipped and divided. A Coker maneuver is then performed.

The surgeon initiates the Coker maneuver near the bile duct, identifying the inferior vena cava. The duodenum is freed from the inferior vena cava in a caudal direction until the left renal vein is visible. The ligament of Treitz is dissected, allowing the jejunum to be advanced under the root of the mesentery and divided. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, and the stomach is lifted ventrally. The dissection proceeds through the gastroepiploic artery and vein, and the duodenum is divided.

The surgeon dissects along the inferior border of the pancreas to identify the pyramesenteric vein. The pancreas is lifted off the pyramesenteric and portal veins, using a hook cautery to divide the tissue. The mesentery is opened for jejunum transection, and the mesentery to the jejunum is divided. The dissection progresses toward the area where the superior mesenteric vein passes under the pancreas. The superior mesenteric artery is skeletonized, and the portal vein is mobilized off the artery to ensure a clean dissection and facilitate lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon frees the gallbladder and dissects across the common hepatic duct, removing the gallbladder en bloc with the specimen. The cystic artery is clipped twice proximally and once distally, followed by clipping of the cystic duct. Dissection continues along the common hepatic duct, and a large branch from the portal vein is clipped. A stent is visible in the bile duct. The specimen is placed into an extraction bag and removed through the applied port site.

The surgeon constructs a bilioenteric anastomosis using a running 3-0 V-lock suture, starting at the nine o'clock position on the bile duct and running the suture inside out of the duct and outside in on the bowel, continuing to the three o'clock position. A two-layer pancreaticojejunostomy is then constructed, with the full thickness of the pancreas brought to two seromuscular purchases of bowel, creating a duct-to-mucosa anastomosis with interrupted sutures. The sutures are tied as placed, and a second layer is completed along the ventral aspect of the pancreas using a running V-lock suture.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis between the pancreatic duct and enterotomy, running the ventral layer using three ov-lock sutures. The posterior and ventral layers are tied at the corners. The staple line on the small bowel is inverted, and the ligament of Treitz is reconstructed with interrupted V-lock sutures. The small bowel and duodenum are opened, excising the staple line on the duodenum. The posterior layer is constructed with sutures placed inside out on the duodenum and outside in on the small bowel, running until reaching three o'clock on the duodenum. The ventral layer reconstruction starts at nine o'clock on the duodenum, sewing to three o'clock, with sutures tied across from each other. A drain is placed under the bile duct, and the anastomoses are wrapped with the falciform ligament.
